request.question: Kettil Karlsson rebelled against the king of Denmark that ruled under what House?

title: Eric II of Denmark
Charles VIII of France
The Royal House of Boureh Gnilane Joof
Christian I of Denmark
Battle of Haraker
Nuhašše
Radbod (prefect)
War of Deposition against King Hans
Kettil Karlsson (Vasa)
Gotarzes II of Parthia
sentences: Eric II the Memorable (Danish: "Erik II Emune" ) (  1090 – 18 September 1137) was king of Denmark between 1134 and 1137.
Eric was an illegitimate son of Eric I of Denmark, who ruled Denmark from 1095 to 1103.
Eric the Memorable rebelled against his uncle Niels of Denmark, and was declared king in 1134.
He punished his adversaries severely, and rewarded his supporters handsomely.
He was killed by a subject in 1137 and was promptly succeeded by his nephew Eric III of Denmark.
Charles VIII, called the Affable, French: "l'Affable" (30 June 1470 – 7 April 1498), was a monarch of the House of Valois who ruled as King of France from 1483 to his death in 1498.
He succeeded his father Louis XI at the age of 13.
His elder sister Anne of France acted as regent jointly with her husband Peter II, Duke of Bourbon until 1491 when the young king turned 21 years of age.
During Anne's regency, the great lords rebelled against royal centralisation efforts in a conflict known as the Mad War (1485-1488), which resulted in a victory for the royal government.

The Royal House of Boureh Gnilane Joof (variation : Mbin Boureh Gnilane in Serer) was a royal house founded in the 14th century by Jaraff Boureh Gnilane Joof (var : "Bouré Gnilane Diouf" or "Buré Ñilaan").
He was a member the Serer tribe, from the pre-colonial Kingdom of Sine now part of independent Senegal.
It was the first royal house founded by the Joof family during the Guelowar period (1350 - 1969).
Boureh Gnilane Joof was a royal prince and a Jaraff (var : "Diaraf"), a with the powers of a Prime Minister.

He was neither a Maad a Sinig (king of Sine) nor a Maad Saloum (king of Saloum) but a royal prince who had the title "Jaraff" bestowed upon him by his cousin and brother-in-law - Maad a Sinig Diessanou Faye (king of Sine).
His father Maad Patar Kholleh Joof (the conqueror) was the king of Laa and Teigne of Baol (king of Baol).
Boureh's brothers were the first from this house to have succeeded to the throne of Sine during the Guelowar period.
His name was adopted in his honour to refer to the first royal house founded by the Joof family during this dynastic period.

The Joof family of Sine, from this royal house also ruled in the Kingdom of Saloum (the Joof paternal dynasty of Sine and Saloum) The Joof family also ruled in Baol (the Joof paternal dynasty of Baol).
From the date of its foundation up to the abolition of the Serer monarchies of Sine and Saloum in 1969, at least ten kings from this house had succeeded to the throne of Sine.

As the first royal house of Sine founded by the Joof family in this dynastic period, the Royal House of Boureh Gnilane Joof holds great significance in Senegambian, Joof family and , because all the subsequent royal houses founded by the Joof family (who ruled in three Senegambian kingdoms) branched out from this royal house.
Christian I (February 1426 – 21 May 1481) was a Scandinavian monarch under the Kalmar Union.
He was King of Denmark (1448–1481), Norway (1450–1481) and Sweden (1457–1464).
From 1460 to 1481, he was also Duke of Schleswig (within Denmark) and Count (after 1474, Duke) of Holstein (within the Holy Roman Empire).
He was the first Danish monarch of the House of Oldenburg.
The Battle of Haraker was fought on 17 April 1464 at the village of Haraker, Västmanland, approximately 20 kilometers north of the city of Västerås in Sweden.
The Swedish separatist army, under the command of the Bishop of Linköping, Kettil Karlsson, defeated King Christian I's Danish army.
Nuhašše, also Nuhašša, was a region in northwestern Syria that flourished in the 2nd millennium BC.
It was a federacy ruled by different kings who collaborated and probably had a high king.
Nuhašše changed hands between different powers in the region such as Egypt, Mitanni and the Hittites.
It rebelled against the latter which led Šuppiluliuma I to attack and annex the region.
Radbod ( 833–54) was the East Frankish prefect of the Eastern March ("marcha orientalis"), the Bavarian frontier towards the Slavs, appointed in 833.
He had been appointed the office after Louis the German's conquest in 828, and subsequent Christianization of the Moravians (828–33).

In 833, according to the "Conversio Bagoariorum et Carantanorum", a Slavic prince, Pribina, had been "driven across the Danube by Mojmir, duke of the Moravians", and fled to Radbod in East Francia around 833.
Radbod introduced him to King Louis the German, who ordered that Pribina should be "instructed in the faith and baptized", and that he serve with his followers in Radbod's army.

Before long, however, Radbod and Pribina fell out, and the latter, fearing for his life, fled with his son Koceľ to the First Bulgarian Empire, and then to Lower Pannonia ruled by a Slavic duke, Ratimir.

Since Lower Pannonia was part of Radbod's prefecture, Ratimir's harboring of Pribina was tantamount to rebellion, therefore, in 838, Louis the German sent Radbod at the head of a large Bavarian army to crush Ratimir, but Pribina and his followers took refuge with the count of Carniola, Salacho.

In short time the latter brokered a reconciliation between Radbod and Pribina, and Louis solved the ongoing instability by appointing Pribina as his faithful "dux" with lands in around the Zala river.
Radbod held contacts with Rastislav (r. 847–70), ruler of the Moravians (successor to Mojmir), who had long posed a danger to Bavaria.

According to the "Annals of St-Bertin", in 853 Charles the Bald, king of West Francia, bribed the Bulgarians to ally with the Slavs (apparently the Moravians) and together attack Louis the German's kingdom.
In the course of the Bulgarian–Moravian attack, Louis the German deposed Radbod in 854 for infidelity, after an uprising.
Radbod then formed a rebel alliance with Rastislav.
In 855, Rastislav (Rastiz) rebelled, and Carloman was made prefect in Radbod's place in 856.
Carloman's 858 campaign forced Rastislav to make peace.

The War of Deposition against King Hans (Swedish: "Avsättningskriget mot kung Hans" ) was a war in which Swedish separatists under the leadership of the Swedish regent Sten Sture the Elder rebelled against the newly elected king of the Kalmar Union, John, King of Denmark.
Kettil Karlsson (Vasa) (c. 1433 – 11 August 1465) was a Swedish clergyman, diplomat, military leader and statesman during the Kalmar Union era.
He was a member of the house of Vasa.
At age 25, he was elected Bishop of Linköping.

He rebelled against King Christian I in 1463, was Captain General "(rikshövitsman)" and "de facto" regent of Sweden from February to August 1464, stepping down during the brief return of King Charles Canutesson from exile.
After falling out with King Charles, Kettil Karlsson was subsequently elected Lord Protector and Regent "(riksföreståndare)" of Sweden from 26 December 1464 to his death.
Gotarzes II of Parthia (Parthian: 𐭂𐭅𐭕𐭓𐭆 "Gōtarz", Ancient Greek Γωτάρζης "Gōtarzēs"; flourished 1st century) was a Prince of Iranian ancestry.
He ruled the Parthian Empire as King intermittently between about 40 to 51.
He was one of the sons of Artabanus III.
When his father died in 38 and his brother Vardanes I succeeded to the throne, Gotarzes II rebelled.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Kettil Karlsson rebelled against the king of Denmark that ruled under what House?
hop1_docs: Kettil Karlsson (Vasa) | Kettil Karlsson (Vasa) (c. 1433 – 11 August 1465) was a Swedish clergyman, diplomat, military leader and statesman during the Kalmar Union era.  He was a member of the house of Vasa.  At age 25, he was elected Bishop of Linköping.  He rebelled against King Christian I in 1463, was Captain General "(rikshövitsman)" and "de facto" regent of Sweden from February to August 1464, stepping down during the brief return of King Charles Canutesson from exile.  After falling out with King Charles, Kettil Karlsson was subsequently elected Lord Protector and Regent "(riksföreståndare)" of Sweden from 26 December 1464 to his death.

Battle of Haraker | The Battle of Haraker was fought on 17 April 1464 at the village of Haraker, Västmanland, approximately 20 kilometers north of the city of Västerås in Sweden.  The Swedish separatist army, under the command of the Bishop of Linköping, Kettil Karlsson, defeated King Christian I's Danish army.

War of Deposition against King Hans | The War of Deposition against King Hans (Swedish: "Avsättningskriget mot kung Hans" ) was a war in which Swedish separatists under the leadership of the Swedish regent Sten Sture the Elder rebelled against the newly elected king of the Kalmar Union, John, King of Denmark.

Eric II of Denmark | Eric II the Memorable (Danish: "Erik II Emune" ) (  1090 – 18 September 1137) was king of Denmark between 1134 and 1137.  Eric was an illegitimate son of Eric I of Denmark, who ruled Denmark from 1095 to 1103.  Eric the Memorable rebelled against his uncle Niels of Denmark, and was declared king in 1134.  He punished his adversaries severely, and rewarded his supporters handsomely.  He was killed by a subject in 1137 and was promptly succeeded by his nephew Eric III of Denmark.

Sidqia | King of Ashkelon in the 8th century BC.  He rebelled against the Assyrian king Sennacherib, just like its neighbor the Kingdom of Judah did.  Sennacherib started a war against these rebels in 701 BC and destroyed the cities Beth-Dagon, Joppa, Banai-Barqa, and Azjuru that were under Siqia's rule.

Čestibor | Čestibor () was a 9th-century King of the Lusatian Serbs (Sorbs).  He was a vassal of Louis the German and in 856 led the Sorbs into battle alongside King Louis against the Glomacze tribe, defeating them and putting them under German rule.  Shortly thereafter in 859, the Sorbs uprise against Čestibor and kill him, rebelling against King Louis.

Gustav I of Sweden | Gustav I, born Gustav Eriksson of the Vasa noble family and later known as Gustav Vasa (12 May 1496 – 29 September 1560), was King of Sweden from 1523 until his death in 1560, previously self-recognised Protector of the Realm ("Riksföreståndare") from 1521, during the ongoing Swedish War of Liberation against King Christian II of Denmark, Norway and Sweden.  Initially of low standing, Gustav rose to lead the rebel movement following the Stockholm Bloodbath, in which his father perished.  Gustav's election as King on 6 June 1523 and his triumphant entry into Stockholm eleven days later meant the end of Medieval Sweden's elective monarchy and the Kalmar Union, and the birth of a hereditary monarchy under the House of Vasa and its successors, including the current House of Bernadotte.
hop1_titles: War of Deposition against King Hans
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Kettil Karlsson — [1]: "Kettil Karlsson (Vasa) (c. 1433 – 11 August 1465)"; [1]: "He rebelled against King Christian I in 1463"
- King Christian I — [1],[2]: [1] "King Christian I"; [2] "King Christian I's Danish army."
- House mention (other actor) — [1]: "member of the house of Vasa."
- Battle context — [2]: "Battle of Haraker"; [2]: "17 April 1464"

Ambiguities:
- "King Christian I" could map to multiple "Christian I" pages in general; context implies the Danish king ("Danish army") but the exact page/title is not given in the passages.

Bridge / missing fact:
- The dynastic House (royal house) of King Christian I (the King of Denmark Kettil Karlsson rebelled against) is not stated in the provided passages.

Retrieval direction:
- Fetch page "Christian I of Denmark" (Wikipedia page title).
- Fetch page "House of Oldenburg" (to confirm Christian I's dynastic house).
- Search query: "Christian I Denmark house" or quoted: "Christian I of Denmark House"
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: List of Christian monasteries in Denmark | The following is a list, as yet incomplete, of Christian religious houses in Denmark whether extant or not, for both men and for women.

Prince Christian of Hesse | Prince Christian of Hesse (Danish: "Christian af Hessen" ; German: "Christian von Hessen" ) (14 August 1776 – 14 November 1814) was a German prince and member of the House of Hesse-Kassel.  As a son of the Danish Field Marshal Prince Charles of Hesse-Kassel and Princess Louise of Denmark, he was a member of the extended Danish Royal Family and spent his entire life in Denmark.

Christian I of Denmark | Christian I (February 1426 – 21 May 1481) was a Scandinavian monarch under the Kalmar Union.  He was King of Denmark (1448–1481), Norway (1450–1481) and Sweden (1457–1464).  From 1460 to 1481, he was also Duke of Schleswig (within Denmark) and Count (after 1474, Duke) of Holstein (within the Holy Roman Empire).  He was the first Danish monarch of the House of Oldenburg.

Christian VII of Denmark | Christian VII (29 January 1749 13 March 1808) was a monarch of the House of Oldenburg who was King of Denmark-Norway and Duke of Schleswig and Holstein from 1766 until his death.  For his motto he chose: ""Gloria ex amore patriae"" ("glory through love of the fatherland").

House of the Holy Ghost, Copenhagen | The House of the Holy Ghost (Danish: Helligåndshuset) in Copenhagen, Denmark is a historic building owned and operated as an exhibition space by the adjacent Church of the Holy Ghost. One of the oldest buildings in Copenhagen, it was part of the largest medieval hospital in Denmark which King Christian I turned into an Augustinian priory 1497.

Prince Andrew of Greece and Denmark | Prince Andrew of Greece and Denmark (Greek: Ανδρέας ; 2 February 1882 (N.S.) – 3 December 1944) of the House of Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg-Glücksburg, was the seventh child and fourth son of King George I of Greece and Olga Constantinovna of Russia.  He was a grandson of Christian IX of Denmark and father of Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh.
Søholm (country house) | Søholm is a listed Neoclassical house overlooking Lake Emdrup in Hellerup in northern Copenhagen, Denmark.  It is from 1809 and was designed by Christian Frederik Hansen.

Prince Andrew of Greece and Denmark
response.summary_2: Kettil Karlsson rebelled against King Christian I, who was the first Danish monarch of the House of Oldenburg.
